dev_plans.content: Lightweight heuristic approach using simple accuracy-gap computation on CelebA validation set. Insert the probe as a validation callback that runs once per epoch after the standard validation loop. Step 1: During validation, collect model predictions (sigmoid > 0.5) for all 40 attributes into a tensor [N, 40], alongside ground-truth labels [N, 40]. Step 2: For each of the three sensitive attributes (indices for Male=20, Young=39, Pale_Skin=26 in CelebA), split the validation set into group_A (attr=1) and group_B (attr=0). Step 3: For each of the remaining 37 target attributes, compute accuracy on each subgroup using (pred==label).float().mean(). Compute abs_gap = |Acc_A - Acc_B|. Step 4: Aggregate by taking the mean of abs_gap across all 37 target attributes for each sensitive axis, then average across the three sensitive axes to get a single scalar. Step 5: Log per-attribute gaps to a CSV (epoch, sensitive_attr, target_attr, acc_A, acc_B, gap) for later inspection, and log the aggregated scalar to TensorBoard/WandB. Step 6: Produce a matplotlib time-series plot of the aggregated gap with the threshold drawn as a red horizontal line. Also emit a heatmap snapshot per epoch showing the 3x37 gap matrix, saved as PNG under runs/<run>/fairness/. The output artifact is (a) a CSV log, (b) a scalar metric per epoch, and (c) a heatmap PNG each epoch. Implementation fits in ~80 lines in a FairnessCallback class.
dev_plans.metric: Mean absolute accuracy gap across 37 non-sensitive attributes, averaged over the three sensitive axes (Male, Young, Pale_Skin), computed on the CelebA validation split per epoch.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.05 — A 5% mean accuracy gap is the healthy-signal cutoff described in the probe. CelebA classifiers trained with standard ResNet-50/18 baselines typically reach 2–4% mean gap after a few epochs on non-gender-correlated attributes, so 0.05 is achievable yet tight enough to flag spurious-correlation regressions. Curves staying below the line indicate healthy training; consistent excursions above suggest subgroup-specific overfitting or demographic shortcut learning.

dev_plans.content: Statistically rigorous approach with bootstrap confidence intervals and equalized-odds-gap computation. Insert the probe at the end of every epoch (post-validation) and also after training completes. Step 1: Cache per-sample predictions and labels for the entire validation set in a numpy memmap, indexed by sample_id. Step 2: For each (sensitive_attr, target_attr) pair where sensitive ∈ {Male, Young, Pale_Skin} and target is one of the remaining 37, compute four quantities on each subgroup: TPR = TP/(TP+FN), FPR = FP/(FP+TN), accuracy, and the equalized-odds gap EO_gap = max(|TPR_A - TPR_B|, |FPR_A - FPR_B|). Step 3: Compute a 95% bootstrap CI for each gap using 1000 resamples stratified by subgroup to account for base-rate differences; store lower/upper bounds. Step 4: Apply a Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction across the 3×37=111 tests at q=0.1 to determine which gaps are statistically significant beyond noise. Step 5: Exclude 'inherently skewed' attributes (Mustache, Goatee, 5_o_Clock_Shadow, Wearing_Lipstick, Heavy_Makeup, No_Beard) from aggregation since base-rate disparity is expected. Step 6: Compute the metric as the mean EO_gap across remaining attribute-sensitive pairs that were flagged significant. Step 7: Persist full report as JSON (per-pair TPR/FPR/acc/CI/p-value) plus a violin plot of the gap distribution per sensitive axis, and a time-series plot of the aggregated metric with threshold line. Emit a structured alert (log.warning + WandB alert hook) if the metric exceeds threshold for 2 consecutive epochs. Artifacts: fairness_report_epoch{n}.json, eo_gap_distribution_epoch{n}.png, fairness_timeseries.png.
dev_plans.metric: Mean equalized-odds gap (max of |ΔTPR|, |ΔFPR|) across non-inherently-skewed target attributes and all three sensitive axes, restricted to pairs significant after BH-FDR correction (q=0.1), computed each epoch.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.10 — The probe specifies >10% gaps on non-gendered attributes as the unhealthy signal. Equalized-odds gaps are typically larger than raw accuracy gaps (they weight the minority class more), and baseline CelebA multi-label classifiers commonly land in the 0.06–0.09 range on filtered attribute sets, making 0.10 an achievable target that a well-tuned run with balanced sampling or reweighting can stay under, while still flagging spurious-correlation regressions per the Gender Shades / Ramaswamy et al. findings.

dev_plans.content: Tooling/library-based approach using Fairlearn + Aequitas for standardized fairness auditing. Insert the probe as a PyTorch Lightning Callback (on_validation_epoch_end) and, for teams on HF Trainer, as a TrainerCallback. Step 1: Add dependencies fairlearn>=0.10, aequitas>=1.0, pandas. During validation, build a pandas DataFrame with columns [sample_id, Male, Young, Pale_Skin, <37 target attrs predictions>, <37 target attrs labels>]. Step 2: For each sensitive attribute, iterate over the 37 target attributes and use fairlearn.metrics.MetricFrame with metrics={'accuracy': accuracy_score, 'tpr': true_positive_rate, 'fpr': false_positive_rate} and sensitive_features=df[sensitive_attr]. Extract .difference(method='between_groups') for each metric to get accuracy_difference, tpr_difference, fpr_difference. Step 3: Use fairlearn.metrics.equalized_odds_difference(y_true, y_pred, sensitive_features=...) as the per-pair scalar. Step 4: Optionally cross-check with Aequitas Bias object (bias.get_disparity_predefined_groups) to produce a standardized disparity report HTML. Step 5: Aggregate by taking the worst-case (maximum) equalized_odds_difference across all 3×37 pairs — this matches fairlearn's recommended worst-group reporting pattern. Step 6: Log to WandB using wandb.Table for per-pair breakdown and wandb.plot.line_series for time-series, plus embed the Aequitas HTML as an artifact. Step 7: Integrate with MLflow or Weights & Biases alerts via fairlearn's threshold gating to auto-fail the run if the metric exceeds threshold for 3 consecutive epochs. Artifacts: fairlearn MetricFrame pickle, aequitas_report.html, wandb Table + line plot, MLflow metric 'fairness/worst_eo_diff'. Code footprint ~150 lines leveraging existing library semantics, giving maintainers a familiar, auditable stack.
dev_plans.metric: Worst-case (maximum) equalized_odds_difference as computed by fairlearn.metrics.equalized_odds_difference across all (sensitive ∈ {Male, Young, Pale_Skin}) × (37 target attributes) pairs on the CelebA validation split, per epoch.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.15 — Worst-case (max over 111 pairs) is strictly larger than the mean gap and will almost always include at least one genuinely skewed pair (e.g., Wearing_Lipstick × Male) that cannot be debiased without counterfactual augmentation. Published Fairlearn-audited CelebA baselines report worst-case EO differences of 0.12–0.18 even with mitigation. 0.15 is therefore a realistic ceiling: reachable by a competently trained and mildly balanced model, while still flagging runs where spurious demographic correlations dominate.
